title: Psychometric Properties of an Arabic Translation of the Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS) in Lebanese Women
abstract: The 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS) measures the construct of breast appreciation, posited as a facet of the broader positive body image construct. To date, there has been limited assessment of the psychometric properties of the BrAS across national and linguistic groups. To add to the literature on breast appreciation, we assessed the psychometric properties of an Arabic translation of the BrAS. An online convenience sample of 626 cisgender women (age range = 18-50 years) from Lebanon completed the Arabic BrAS along with additional measures of body image, disordered eating, and psychological well-being. Across two split-half subsamples (n = 312 and n = 314, respectively), exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis showed that it was possible to extract a unidimensional model of the BrAS with all nine items. This model of the BrAS had adequate composite reliability in both split-half subsamples. Additionally, the BrAS evidenced good patterns of convergent (positive associations with other indices of positive body image and breast awareness, and negative associations with body dissatisfaction and symptoms of disordered eating) and concurrent validity (positive relationships with gratitude and compassionate self-responding). These results suggest that the Arabic translation of the BrAS retains its robust psychometric properties in Lebanese women.
text:
Psychometric properties of an Arabic translation of the Breast Appreciation 
Scale (BrAS) in Lebanese women
Souheil Hallit a,b,*, Jennifer Todd c,d, Feten Fekih-Romdhane e,f, Sahar Obeid g, Mirna Fawaz h,  
Nour El Achkar a, Diana Malaeb i, Viren Swami c,d,**
a School of Medicine and Medical Sciences, Holy Spirit University of Kaslik, Jounieh, Lebanon
b Applied Science Research Center, Applied Science Private University, Amman, Jordan
c School of Psychology, Sport, and Sensory Sciences, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, United Kingdom
d Centre for Psychological Medicine, Perdana University, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
e Faculty of Medicine of Tunis, Tunis Al Manar University, Tunis, Tunisia
f The Tunisian Center of Early Intervention in Psychosis, Department of Psychiatry Ibn Omrane, Razi Hospital, Tunis, Tunisia
g School of Arts and Sciences, Social and Education Sciences Department, Lebanese American University, Jbeil, Lebanon
h School of Nursing, Beirut Arab University, Beirut, Lebanon
i College of Pharmacy, Gulf Medical University, Ajman, United Arab Emirates
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Breast appreciation
Breasted experience
Positive body image
Test adaptation
Lebanon
A B S T R A C T
The 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS) measures the construct of breast appreciation, posited as a facet of 
the broader positive body image construct. To date, there has been limited assessment of the psychometric 
properties of the BrAS across national and linguistic groups. To add to the literature on breast appreciation, we 
assessed the psychometric properties of an Arabic translation of the BrAS. An online convenience sample of 626 
cisgender women (age range = 18–50 years) from Lebanon completed the Arabic BrAS along with additional 
measures of body image, disordered eating, and psychological well-being. Across two split-half subsamples 
(n = 312 and n = 314, respectively), exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis showed that it was possible to 
extract a unidimensional model of the BrAS with all nine items. This model of the BrAS had adequate composite 
reliability in both split-half subsamples. Additionally, the BrAS evidenced good patterns of convergent (positive 
associations with other indices of positive body image and breast awareness, and negative associations with body 
dissatisfaction and symptoms of disordered eating) and concurrent validity (positive relationships with gratitude 
and compassionate self-responding). These results suggest that the Arabic translation of the BrAS retains its 
robust psychometric properties in Lebanese women.
1. Introduction
Although scholars generally agree that “breasted experiences” (cf. 
Young, 1992) play an important role in shaping (cisgender) women’s 
body image (e.g., Millsted & Frith, 2003; Swami & Furnham, 2018), 
relevant research has historically focused on breast size dissatisfaction 
(e.g., Lombardo et al., 2019; Swami et al., 2015; Swami et al., 2020) and 
broader body image outcomes in breast cancer survivors (e.g., Helms 
et al., 2008). While such research remains valuable, particularly in view 
of sociocultural discourse that continues to commodify, fetishise, and 
objectify the breasts (Gerald & Potvin, 2009; Swami & Tov´ee, 2013), the 
focus on negative experiences risks missing the complexities of breasted 
experiences (Lee, 1997; Nash, 2014). For example, Young (1992)
described how – from the perspective of women themselves and in the 
absence of a male gaze – women’s sensory experiences of their breasts 
(e.g., how they move, how sensitive they are) matter more than aes­
thetics. In addition, in women’s narratives, the breasts are also often 
identified as sites of pleasure (Young, 1992), sexual authority (Lee, 
1997), and joy (McCann, 2020).
Motivated by the broader shift in the body image literature towards 
understanding aspects of positive body image (for reviews, see Daniels 
et al., 2018; Tylka & Piran, 2019), scholars have also recently begun to 
examine positive breasted experiences in cisgender women. Such ex­
periences can be wide-ranging, encompassing feelings of maternal joy,

E-mail addresses: souheilhallit@usek.edu.lb (S. Hallit), viren.swami@aru.ac.uk (V. Swami). 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Body Image
journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/body-image
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bodyim.2026.102031
Received 13 August 2025; Received in revised form 20 November 2025; Accepted 8 January 2026  
Body Image 56 (2026) 102031 
Available online 13 January 2026 
1740-1445/© 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). 
child nurturance, and positive femininity derived from one’s breasts 
(Millsted & Frith, 2003; Schmied & Barclay, 1999). However, one spe­
cific form of positive breasted experience that has received scholarly 
attention is breast appreciation, which Swami, Todd, and Tylka (2022, p. 
277) defined as “accepting, holding favourable opinions toward, and 
respecting one’s breasts and their functions, while also rejecting 
media-promoted breast ideals as the only form of beauty”. In the view of 
Swami, Todd, and Tylka (2022), breast appreciation is a facet of the 
broader positive body image construct, analogous to other facets such as 
body appreciation (Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015) and functionality 
appreciation (Alleva et al., 2017).
To measure the construct of breast appreciation, Swami, Todd, and 
Tylka (2022) developed the 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS). 
Based on data from samples of cisgender women in the United Kingdom, 
exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses supported the retention of 
a unidimensional model of the BrAS. This unidimensional model was 
found to be scalar invariant across mothers and non-mothers, as well 
across national groups (i.e., cisgender women from the UK, United 
States, and Australia). Swami, Todd, and Tylka (2022) also reported that 
the BrAS evidenced adequate 4-week test-retest reliability, as well as 
adequate convergent validity (e.g., positive associations with indices of 
positive body image), concurrent validity (e.g., positive associations 
with psychological well-being), discriminant validity (i.e., no significant 
association with impression management), and incremental validity 
(breast appreciation scores predicted unique variance in breast aware­
ness above-and-beyond associations with breast size dissatisfaction).
To date, only two studies have assessed the psychometric properties 
of the BrAS outside English-speaking populations. Specifically, the 9- 
item unidimensional model of the BrAS was found to have adequate 
EFA- and CFA-based fit in cisgender samples of women from Romania 
(Swami et al., 2024) and Colombia (Mebarak et al., 2025). Both studies 
also reported that the unidimensional model of the BrAS achieved 
complete invariance (i.e., through to latent mean invariance) across 
mothers and non-mothers, had adequate composite reliability, and 
showed adequate evidence of convergent and concurrent validity. 
Additionally, Mebarak et al. (2025) also reported that BrAS scores 
remained temporally stable after four weeks. These results may be taken 
as evidence that the BrAS retains its robust psychometric properties in 
diverse national and linguistic contexts, but there remains a need to 
include further cultural and linguistic groups. This is important given 
that breasted experiences are likely shaped by a range of cultural and 
national factors, including patriarchal systems, cultural norms and 
values, idealised femininity and sexuality, heteronormativity, and the 
racialisation of beauty (Naugler, 2009; Swami et al., 2020).
1.1. The Lebanese context
To add to the emergent literature on breast appreciation in diverse 
cultural and linguistic groups, we sought to examine the psychometric 
properties of an Arabic translation of the BrAS in a sample of cisgender 
women from Lebanon. There are several reasons why this work may be 
of theoretical and practical interest to other scholars. First, development 
of a psychometrically valid Arabic translation of the BrAS would add to 
the growing inventory of instruments available to scholars in the Arabic- 
speaking world – primarily encompassing North Africa and the Middle 
East – for the assessment of indices of positive body image (e.g., Swami, 
Todd, Azzi et al., 2022; Swami et al., 2023). Indeed, despite recent ad­
vancements in the Middle East generally and in Lebanon specifically (e. 
g., Awad et al., 2024; Haddad et al., 2019), there remains a dearth of 
research on aspects of positive body image in Arabic-speaking pop­
ulations. Second, Arab cultures and linguistics may shape breasted ex­
periences in somewhat unique ways.
Historically, Arabic cultures exalted women’s breasts as highly erotic 
(e.g., the breast features prominently in classical Arabic poetry; Drozdík, 
1997). At the same time, however, popular epics and religious stories 
also venerated women’s breasts for their role in child nurturance. In 
many prophetic stories, for instance, mother figures are able to wield 
sizeable influence over their families and social worlds through their 
capacity to nurse (Schine, 2019). Over time, however, this coupling of 
the breasts with their functional purposes appears to have largely been 
lost or weakened across Arabic cultures. For instance, in their 
article-diary discussing motherhood and breast cancer, Alatrash and 
Zarif (2025) have noted how the Arabic word for “mother” (  )ﻡﺃis pri­
marily used as a noun, with only rare instances of its use as a verb. Such 
linguistic limitations may entrench patriarchal norms that increasingly 
constrain the role and influence of motherhood in Arabic cultures 
(Ghazal, 2015), while simultaneously emphasising the aesthetic value of 
breasts.
The cultural shift toward emphasis on the aesthetic and symbolic 
value of the breast in Arabic cultures may also have roots elsewhere. For 
instance, some scholars have attributed this shift to the influence of 
European cultural imperialism, which sought to “orientalise” and 
objectify Arab women (see Mehdid, 1993). This has had a lasting legacy 
on the ways in which women in the Arab world navigate bodily expe­
riences and hypersexual appearance ideals within broader socioeco­
nomic and political contexts. In contemporary Lebanon, for instance, 
Melki et al. (2024) have discussed how cultural imperialism and het­
eronormative patriarchal systems have erased localised representations 
of women. For many young Lebanese women – irrespective of their 
communal and class identities – the breasts have become a site open to 
“investment” through breast augmentation for the sake of social and 
economic advancement (Doherty, 2008; Younes, 2019), a narrative 
fostered by the Lebanese state’s neoliberal approach to body subjectivity 
(El Dine, 2025).
Beyond these issues, there is also a practical reason why develop­
ment of an Arabic version of the BrAS may be useful. Specifically, Leb­
anese women have some of the highest rates of breast cancer incidence 
and mortality in the Middle East (Fares et al., 2019; Lakkis et al., 2010). 
Further, although the Lebanese Ministry of Public Health has facilitated 
access to mammography screenings for women aged 40 years and above 
across Lebanon since 2002 (Sbaity et al., 2011), there are several bar­
riers to uptake, including poor knowledge and awareness of breast 
cancer (Doumit et al., 2017). As such, there may be value in promoting 
breast awareness (i.e., familiarity with one’s breasts and the confidence 
to notice and detect changes to the breasts throughout a woman’s life; 
McCready et al., 2005; Thornton & Pillarisetti, 2008) as a means of 
promoting improved breast cancer detection in Lebanese women. Given 
that breast appreciation is positively associated with breast awareness 
(Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022), it is likely that the availability of a 
validated measure of breast appreciation would benefit clinical practice 
in the context of Lebanon.
1.2. The present study
In view of the issues above, the present study sought to assess the 
psychometric properties of an Arabic translation of the BrAS in a sample 
of cisgender women from Lebanon. As in previous work (Mebarak et al., 
2025; Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022; Swami et al., 2024), we used an 
EFA-to-CFA strategy, which allowed us to first establish the most suit­
able model of BrAS scores for our data without the imposition of 
modelling constraints (i.e., using EFA) and then to cross-validate and 
confirm the EFA-derived model (i.e., using CFA; Swami et al., 2021; 
Swami & Barron, 2019). We hypothesised that scores on the Lebanese 
version of the BrAS would reduce to a unidimensional model with all 
nine items retained, which would be consistent with previous work 
(Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022; Swami et al., 2024).
In addition, we also assessed construct validity of the BrAS in our 
sample. Selection of variables to assess the nomological network of the 
BrAS was guided by significant associations reported in previous work 
(Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022; Swami et al., 2024), 
relevant theoretical models of positive body image (e.g., Homan & 
Tylka, 2018), and the availability of instruments that have been 
S. Hallit et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
Body Image 56 (2026) 102031 
2 
validated for use in Arabic-speaking populations. Specifically, to assess 
convergent validity, we examined associations between breast appreci­
ation and indices of positive body image (body appreciation, function­
ality appreciation), symptoms of disordered eating, and breast 
awareness (operationalised using an instrument that we concurrently 
translated), with the expectation of small-to-moderate associations. To 
assess concurrent validity, we also examined associations with 
self-compassion 
and 
gratitude, 
with 
the 
expectation 
of 
small-to-moderate associations.
2. Method
2.1. Participants
The initial participant pool consisted of an online convenience 
sample of 656 cisgender women, but we excluded responses from 30 
individuals who were missing > 80% of survey data. The final sample, 
therefore, consisted of 626 cisgender women who ranged in age from 18 
to 50 years (M = 25.99, SD = 9.47, Mdn 
= 22.00, interquartile range =
8). Of the total sample, most were single (72.0 %), while smaller pro­
portions were either married (22.5 %) or divorced (5.4 %). In terms of 
educational qualifications, 1.4 % had completed primary schooling, 
15.0 % had completed secondary schooling, and 83.5 % had a university 
degree. No additional demographic data were collected.
2.2. Measures
2.2.1. Breast appreciation
Participants were asked to complete a novel Arabic translation of the 
9-item BrAS (Swami et al., 2022). To prepare this Arabic translation 
from its original in English, we used a combination of the 
back-translation method and a committee approach (Swami & Barron, 
2019). First, two bilingual translators – one informed, and one unin­
formed – independently forward-translated the BrAS instructions, items, 
and response options into Arabic. Next, the two translations were 
examined by a third, independent bilingual translator who resolved any 
discrepancies and produce a synthesised translation. In a third step, the 
synthesised translation was back-translated by two bilingual translators 
naïve to the BrAS back into English. Finally, the forward- and back- 
translations were compared by an expert committee comprising all the 
translators, a methodologist, a linguist, two of the original developers of 
the BrAS, who resolved several minor inconsistencies between versions. 
We did not conduct a pilot test of understanding in the present case. The 
items of the BrAS in English and Arabic are presented in Table 1. 
Consistent with the original BrAS, all items were rated on a 5-point scale 
ranging from 1 (strongly disagree; Arabic:  ﻻﺃﻭﺍﻓﻖﺑﺸﺪﺓ
 )to 5 (strongly agree; 
Arabic:  ﻻﺃﻭﺍﻓﻖﺑﺸﺪﺓ
 .)Higher item-level scores reflect greater breast 
appreciation.
2.2.2. Body appreciation
The survey package also included the Body Appreciation Scale-2 
(BAS-2; Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015; Arabic translation: Vally 
et al., 2019). This 10-item instrument assesses acceptance of one’s body, 
respect and care for one’s body, and protection of one’s body from un­
realistic beauty standards (sample item: “I respect my body”. All items 
were rated on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (never) to 5 (always), with 
higher scores reflective of greater body appreciation. Scores on the 
Arabic version of the BAS-2 have been shown to have a unidimensional 
factor structure with adequate composite reliability and convergent 
validity (Swami et al., 2023), including in Lebanese women 
(Fekih-Romdhane et al., 2023). In the present study, composite reli­
ability as assessed using McDonald’s ω for BAS-2 scores was .97 (95 % CI 
= .96, .98).
2.2.3. Functionality appreciation
Participants were asked to complete the Functionality Appreciation 
Scale (FAS; Alleva et al., 2017; Arabic translation: Swami et al., 2022). 
This 7-item instruments measures one’s appreciation of what the body 
does and can do (sample item: “I appreciate my body for what it is 
capable of doing”). All items were rated on a 5-point scale, ranging from 
1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree), with higher scores reflecting 
greater functionality appreciation. Scores on the Arabic version of the 
FAS have been shown to have a unidimensional factor structure, 
adequate composite reliability, and adequate convergent and concur­
rent validity in Lebanese women (Swami et al., 2022). McDonald’s ω for 
FAS scores was .96 (95 % CI = .96, .97).
2.2.4. Body dissatisfaction
To measure body dissatisfaction, we used the Body Dissatisfaction 
subscale of the Eating Disorders Inventory-3 (EDI-BD; Garner, 2004; 
Arabic translation: Haddad et al., 2019). This is a 9-itam instrument that 
measures dissatisfaction with various body parts (sample item: “I think 
that my stomach is too big”), with items were rated on a 5-point scale 
(1 = never, 5 = always). Although nominally measuring a singular un­
derlying construct (Garner, 2004), factor analytic results with Lebanese 
adults has shown that scores on the subscale reduce to two dimensions 
(Gerges et al., 2023), likely an artefact of including positive and nega­
tively valenced items in the same instrument. As such, we computed an 
index of body dissatisfaction using the five negatively valenced items 
only, with higher scores reflective of greater body dissatisfaction. 
McDonald’s ω for EDI-BD scores was .85 (95 % CI = .83, .87).
2.2.5. Symptoms of disordered eating
Participants were also asked to complete a short-form, 7-item version 
of the Eating Attitudes Test (Garner et al., 1982) in Arabic (EAT-7; 
Fekih-Romdhane et al., 2022). This short form of the instrument assesses 
symptoms and concerns characteristic of food- and eating-related 
disordered behaviours (sample item: “I avoid eating when I am hun­
gry”). All items were rated on a 6-point scale ranging from 1 (never) to 6 
(always), with higher scores reflective of characteristics of disordered 
eating. Scores on the EAT-7 have been shown to have a unidimensional 
factor structure and adequate convergent validity in Lebanese women 
(Fekih-Romdhane et al., 2022). In the present study, McDonald’s ω for 
EAT-7 scores was .87 (95 % CI = .86, .89).

Body Image 56 (2026) 102031 
3 
2.2.6. Gratitude
To measure the construct of gratitude, participants were asked to 
complete the Gratitude Questionnaire-6 (GQ-6; McCullough et al., 2002; 
Arabic translation: Fekih-Romdhane et al., 2025). In its original version, 
this instrument consists of 6 items assessing one’s disposition toward 
gratitude (sample item: “I have so much in life to be thankful for”). In 
Arabic, however, only a 4-item version that excludes two negatively 
worded items has been shown to have adequate factor analytic fit (GQ-4; 
Fekih-Romdhane et al., 2025). As such, we computed gratitude using 
this 4-item version, with higher scores reflective of greater gratitude. All 
items were rated on a 7-point scale ranging from 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 
(strongly agree). The 4-item version of the Gratitude Questionnaire has 
been shown to have adequate concurrent validity (Fekih-Romdhane 
et al., 2025). In the present study, McDonald’s ω for this instrument was 
.95 (95 % CI = .95, .96).
2.2.7. Self-compassion
Self-compassion was assessed using the 12-item Self-Compassion 
Scale-Short Form (SCS-SF; Raes et al., 2011; Arabic translation: Swami 
et al., 2025). The SCS-SF uses two items per component to measure six 
hypothesised components of self-compassion, namely self-kindness, 
mindfulness, common humanity, self-judgement, overidentification, 
and isolation (sample item: “When something upsets me, I try to keep my 
emotions in balance”). All items were rated on a 5-point scale, ranging 
from 1 (almost never) to 5 (almost always). Recent work, including with 
Lebanese adults, has shown that scores on the scale are best con­
ceptualised as two distinct factors assessing compassionate (five items) 
and uncompassionate self-responding (6 items), respectively (Swami 
et al., 2025). Thus, for the present purposes, we computed an index of 
compassionate self-responding using the five relevant items. Higher 
scores 
are, 
therefore, 
reflective 
of 
greater 
compassionate 
self-responding. This variant of the SCS-SF has been shown to have 
adequate psychometric properties in diverse samples, including Leb­
anese adults (Swami et al., 2025). In the present study, McDonald’s ω for 
this instrument was.88 (95 % CI = .86, .89).
2.2.8. Breast awareness
To measure breast awareness, we used the measure of breast 
awareness developed by Swami, Todd, and Tylka (2022). This 3-item 
instrument assesses familiarity with one’s breasts and the confidence 
to notice and detect changes to the breasts throughout one’s life (sample 
item: “I have a good idea of how my breasts will change over my life­
time”). An Arabic version of this instrument was developed using the 
same 5-step translational strategy as described above (see Supplemen­
tary Table S1 for items in Arabic and English). In the main survey, all 
items were rated on a 5-point scale (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly 
agree). Because this instrument has not been previously used in Arabic, 
we subjected our data to an EFA to assess its factor structure. Our results 
showed that it was possible to extract a single factor with all three items, 
common variance among the items was deflated (indicated by a low 
Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin value; see Supplementary Materials). On this basis, 
we computed an index of breast awareness using all three items, with 
higher scores reflecting greater breast awareness. In the present study, 
McDonald’s ω for this instrument was .78 (95 % CI = .74, .81).
2.2.9. Demographics
Participants were asked to provide their demographic details con­
sisting of age, highest educational qualification, and marital status.
2.3. Procedures
Once ethics approval for this study was obtained from the Hospital of 
Rayak (approval code: ECO-R-616), the study was advertised on social 
media. Inclusion criteria for participation including self-identifying as a 
cisgender woman, a resident and citizen of Lebanon, being of adult age, 
and fluent in Arabic. We also excluded women who were pregnant at the 
time of the study, who had a history of breast cancer, and who had breast 
augmentation surgery. Potential participants who were eligible for in­
clusion provided digital informed consent before completing an online 
survey hosted on Google Forms. All data were collected between March 
and August 2025, and the order of presentation of the instruments 
described above was pre-randomised for each participant. To support 
the collection of high-quality data, we used CAPTCHA verification to 
prevent responses from bots and restricted survey submissions to one per 
IP address (to avoid duplicate entries). The survey was anonymous and 
participants completed the survey voluntarily and without remunera­
tion. All participants received debriefing information at the end of the 
survey.
2.4. Analytic strategy
2.4.1. Data treatment
There were no missing data in the retained dataset. To examine the 
factor structure of the BrAS, we used an EFA-to-CFA strategy as rec­
ommended by Swami and Barron (2019). To ensure adequate sample 
sizes for both EFA and CFA, we split the sample using a 
computer-generated random seed, resulting in one split-half for EFA 
(n = 312) and a second split-half for CFA (n = 314). There were no sig­
nificant differences across the two split-halves in terms of mean age, t 
(624) = 0.61, p = .544, d = .05, as well as the distribution of marital 
status, χ(2) = 6.14, p = .058, and education qualifications, χ(2) = 1.47, 
p = .689.
2.4.2. Exploratory factor analysis
Data from the first split-half were subjected to principal-axis EFA 
using the psych package (Revelle, 2019) in R (R Development Core 
Team, 2014). Our subsample size satisfied Worthington and Whittaker’s 
(2006) item-communality requirements, as well as assumptions for EFA 
based on item distributions, average item correlations, and item-total 
correlations (Clark & Watson, 1995). As per Hair et al. (2009), data 
factorability was assessed using the Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure 
of sampling adequacy (which should ideally be ≥.80) and Bartlett’s test 
of sphericity (which should be significant). Principal-axis factoring was 
used for the EFAs as it yields results similar to commonly used maximum 
likelihood estimation without assuming multivariate normality 
(Goretzko et al., 2021). Given the expectation of a single orthogonal 
factor, a quartimax rotation was applied (Pedhazur & Schmelkin, 1991).
To estimate the number of factors to extract and factor structure 
adequacy, we considered the results of a parallel analysis test (Horn, 
1965) and examined fit statistics using commonly used fit indices 
(Finch, 2020; Swami & Barron, 2019). Specifically, we used the normed 
model chi-square (χ²/df; values < 3.0 considered indicative of good fit), 
the Steiger-Lind root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA) and 
its 90 % CI (values close to .06 considered to be indicative of good fit and 
up to about .08 indicative of adequate fit), the standardised root mean 
square residual (SRMR; values < .09 indicative of good fit), the 
Tucker-Lewis index (TLI; values close to or > .95 indicative of good fit), 
and the comparative fit index (CFI; values close to or > .95 indicative of 
adequate fit) (Hu & Bentler, 1999). Corrections to fit indices were not 
required as EFA is robust to violations of univariate and multivariate 
normality (Curran et al., 1996). Item retention was based on the 
recommendation that items with “fair” loadings and above (i.e., ≥.33) 
and with low inter-item correlations (suggestive of low item redun­
dancy) as indicated by the anti-image correlation matrix should be 
retained (Comrey & Lee, 1992).
2.4.3. Confirmatory factor analysis
We used data from the second split-half to conduct a CFA using the 
lavaan (Rosseel, 2012), semTools (Jorgensen et al., 2018), and MVN 
packages (Korkmaz et al., 2014) with R (R Development Core Team, 
2014). Based on proactive Monte Carlo simulations, Swami et al. (2024)
previously reported that a sample size of about 160 would be sufficient 
S. Hallit et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
Body Image 56 (2026) 102031 
4 
for this analysis, which was surpassed in our subsample. Our aim was to 
test the unidimensional model proposed by Swami, Todd, and Tylka 
(2022) and, if discrepant, the model suggested by our EFA results. 
Assessment of the data for univariate normality indicated acceptable 
levels of skewness (values ranged from −0.77 to −0.35) and kurtosis 
(values ranged from −0.12 to –0.32), but assessment of multivariate 
normality indicated that the data were not multivariate normal (Mar­
dia’s skewness = 1208.38, p < .001, Mardia’s kurtosis = 59.48, 
p < .001). Therefore, parameter estimates were obtained using the 
robust maximum likelihood method and fit indices (see Section 2.4.2) 
were interpreted with the Satorra-Bentler correction applied (Satorra & 
Bentler, 2001).
2.4.4. Composite reliability
Composite reliability in all analyses was assessed using McDonald’s 
ω and its associated 95 % CI, with values greater than .70 reflecting 
adequate internal reliability (Dunn et al., 2014). Specifically, we 
computed ω using the semTools package for R (Jorgensen et al., 2018), 
which estimates reliability based on CFA parameters and allows for 
models that do not fit the data perfectly (Kelley & Pornprasertmanit, 
2016).
2.4.5. Construct validity
With data from the second split-half subsample, we assessed 
discriminant validity using the Fornell-Larcker criterion (Fornell & 
Larcker, 1981), with average variance extracted (AVE) values of ≥.50 
considered adequate and meaning that a latent variable is able to 
explain more than half of the variance of its indicators on average (i.e., 
items converge into a uniform construct). To assess construct validity (i. 
e., convergent and concurrent validity), we used the total sample to 
construct a structural equation model (SEM) in which the BrAS factor 
structure was estimated based on model providing the most optimal 
representation of the data across both split-half subsamples. In this 
model, the latent factors of breast appreciation, body appreciation, 
functionality appreciation, body dissatisfaction, disordered eating 
symptoms, gratitude, compassionate self-responding, and breast 
awareness were all correlated. We also report bivariate Spearman cor­
relations with manifest (observed) scores for completeness. Based on 
Cohen (1992), values ≤.10 were considered weak, ~.30 were consid­
ered moderate, and ~.50 were considered strong correlations.
3. Results
3.1. Exploratory factor analysis
Both Bartlett’s test of sphericity, χ2(36) = 2589.90, p < .001, and the 
KMO measure of sampling adequacy (.94), indicated that the BrAS items 
had adequate common variance for factor analysis in the first split-half 
subsample. Principal-axis EFA indicated that a single factor, which 
explained 69.1 % of common variance, should be extracted. This was 
confirmed by the parallel analysis test (λ1 = 6.23; λ2 = 0.58 < 0.98). The 
fit indices for this model were: χ2(27) = 180.32, p < .001, CFI = .941, 
TLI = .921, RMSEA = .135 (90 % CI = .117, .154), SRMR = .033. Factor 
loadings, reported in Table 1, indicated that all nine items should be 
retained. In this subsample, BrAS scores had adequate composite reli­
ability (ω = .95, 95 % CI = .94, .96).
3.2. Confirmatory factor analysis
CFA fit indices indicated that fit of the unidimensional model of BrAS 
scores was acceptable: SBχ2(27) = 67.32, p < .001, SBχ²normed = 2.49, 
robust RMSEA = .069 (90 % CI = .056, .082), SRMR = .043, robust CFI 
= .949, robust TLI = .932. The standardised estimates of factor loadings 
were all adequate (see Table 1). In this subsample, BrAS scores had 
adequate composite reliability (ω = .94, 95 % CI = .92, .95).
3.3. Construct Validity
Discriminant validity for the unidimensional model of BrAS was 
adequate, as AVE = .63. In terms of convergent and concurrent validity, 
evaluation of the goodness-of-fit statistics for the SEM including latent 
scores for all measures included in the survey indicated acceptable 
model fit in the total sample, χ2(1196) = 2098.02, p < .001, robust CFI 
= .953, TLI = .950, robust RMSEA = .041 (90 % CI = .038, .044), SRMR 
= .045. As reported in Table 2, latent breast appreciation scores had 
moderate-to-strong positive correlations with latent scores for breast 
awareness, body appreciation, functionality appreciation, gratitude, and 
compassionate self-responding, and small-to-moderate negative associ­
ations with body dissatisfaction and symptoms of disordered eating. The 
same pattern of results was observed when examining associations with 
manifest scores, which were based on the computation of mean values 
(see Table 2).
4. Discussion
In the present study, our objective was to assess the psychometric 
properties of a novel Arabic translation of the BrAS (Swami, Todd, & 
Tylka, 2022) in a sample of Lebanese cisgender women. In terms of 
factorial validity, we adopted an EFA-to-CFA analytic strategy, which 
showed that it was possible to retain a unidimensional model of the 
BrAS. This model included all nine of the original BrAS items and 
showed adequate composite reliabilities across both split-half sub­
samples. Our results also supported the construct (i.e., convergent and 
concurrent) validity of the Arabic BrAS, insofar as (latent and manifest) 
associations with additional constructs were in the expected directions. 
In broad outline, these results are consistent with earlier test adaptation 
studies, which have shown the BrAS has adequate psychometric prop­
erties in Romanian-speaking women from Romania (Swami et al., 2024) 
and Spanish-speaking women from Colombia (Mebarak et al., 2025).
Turning first to factorial validity, the results of our EFA and parallel 
analysis in the first split-half subsample suggested that a single BrAS 
factor should be extracted. This unidimensional model included all nine 
original BrAS items with item-factor loadings that were consistently 
good. In addition, our cross-validation with CFA in the second split-half 
subsample showed that the unidimensional model of the BrAS has 
adequate fit to our data, with standardised estimates of factor loadings 
again consistently good. Additionally, composite reliability was 
adequate in both split-half subsamples. Taken together, these results 
suggests that the Arabic BrAS can be reliably considered to be repre­
sented by a unidimensional model consisting of all nine items. This is 
consistent with the results of the parent development study (Swami, 
Todd, & Tylka, 2022), as well as previous test adaptation work (Mebarak 
et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2024).
In the present study, we also examined the construct validity of the 
Arabic BrAS. In terms of convergent validity, we found that latent BrAS 
scores were moderately and positively associated with latent body 
appreciation and functionality appreciation. This is consistent with the 
claim that breast appreciation can be considered to be a facet of the

Body Image 56 (2026) 102031 
5 
broader positive body image construct (Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022). 
Additional support for convergent validity came from the finding that 
breast appreciation was moderately and negatively associated with body 
dissatisfaction and symptoms of disordered eating. In terms of concur­
rent validity, we found that latent breast appreciation was positively and 
moderately associated with gratitude and compassionate self- 
responding. In broad outline, these findings are consistent with evi­
dence of convergent and concurrent validity of the BrAS presented 
elsewhere (Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami, Todd, & Tylka, 2022; Swami 
et al., 2024).
Finally, and again supporting the convergent validity of the BrAS, we 
found that a moderate and positive association between latent breast 
appreciation and breast awareness. This is an important finding in its 
own right because, as we noted earlier, Lebanese women have some of 
the highest rates of breast cancer incidence and mortality in the Middle 
East (Fares et al., 2019; Lakkis et al., 2010). One way of potentially 
reducing the incidence rate of breast cancer is through the promotion of 
breast awareness (McCready et al., 2005; Thornton & Pillarisetti, 2008). 
In view of the positive association between these constructs in the pre­
sent study, it might be suggested that Lebanese women may benefit in 
terms of breast cancer detection from the promotion of greater breast 
appreciation and its downstream impact on breast awareness (Swami, 
Todd, & Tylka, 2022), although of course any causal conclusions should 
be treated with caution.
Of note, however, the measure of breast awareness used in the pre­
sent study – which we obtained from Swami, Todd, and Tylka (2022) – 
showed evidence of deflated common variance among the three items. 
This is consistent with previous work (Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami 
et al., 2024) and suggests that this instrument may be assessing disparate 
components of a broader breast awareness construct. While we have 
used this instrument with caution in the present study, we recommend 
that scholars consider revising this instrument or developing an 
improved measure of breast awareness in the future. This would help to 
provide greater confidence that the association between breast appre­
ciation and breast awareness, or components of breast awareness, is 
indeed reliable and valid. Meanwhile, the results presented in Supple­
mentary Materials suggest that the existing measure of breast awareness 
may provide a unidimensional assessment of the construct in Lebanese 
women.
There are several other limitations of the present study, which should 
be addressed in future work. First, we did not include a measure of 
breast dissatisfaction (e.g., Swami et al., 2015), which would have 
provided an additional assessment of convergent validity and would 
have allowed us to test for incremental validity (Mebarak et al., 2025). 
In future work, it may also be useful to examine indices of discriminant 
validity, as well as temporal stability of BrAS scores. In a similar vein, we 
also neglected to ask participants about any infant feeding practices and 
motherhood status (i.e., the number of children they had given birth to). 
Swami, Tylka, and Todd (2022) suggested that higher breast apprecia­
tion in mothers may be taken as evidence of known-groups validity, but 
more recent work has suggested that there are no significant differences 
between mothers and non-mothers in latent breast appreciation 
(Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2024). Nevertheless, this may be an 
interesting aspect for future research, particularly given the pressure 
that Lebanese women experience to become mothers and the anxiety 
they experience when they remain childless (Kaak et al., 2025).
In future work, it may also be important to consider the ways that 
demographic diversity affect breast appreciation. For instance, scholars 
have discussed how religious identities, class structures, and neoliber­
alism have intersected to exaggerate communal identities that may have 
an impact on body image among women in Lebanon (Deeb, 2006; El 
Dine, 2025). As such, it may be useful to consider whether there are 
differences in breast appreciation between Lebanese Christians and 
Sunni Muslims (who have historically held greater political power) and 
Shia Muslims (who have historically been politically marginalised and 
tend to be more conservative in terms of religion). In a similar vein, it 
may also be useful to consider the role that food insecurity plays in 
shaping breast appreciation, particularly given evidence that Lebanese 
women are disproportionately burdened by food deprivation (e.g., 
likelihood of engaging in self-sacrificial behaviour) and its impacts on 
psychological well-being (Mattar et al., 2023).
Finally, there are some more generic limitations that should be 
considered when assessing our findings. For example, our recruitment 
method means that our sample is unlikely to have been representative of 
the wider Lebanese population of women. Indeed, our sample was 
relatively young, highly educated, and predominantly single, which may 
mean that their experiences of breast appreciation have limited gen­
eralisability in the Lebanese context. We also did not request residential 
information and, given our reliance on online recruitment, we cannot 
entirely rule out geographic clustering (e.g., recruitment being 
concentrated in particular locales), which may have affected responding 
on our survey. In a similar vein, the focus of our work was on cisgender 
women and it may be useful in future work to investigate the applica­
bility of the BrAS to understanding breasted experiences in populations 
of other gender identities (e.g., transwomen and people assigned as fe­
male at birth with breasts but who not identify as women). Finally, it 
should not be assumed that our findings will generalise to other Arabic- 
speaking populations. Rather, the psychometric properties of the Arabic 
BrAS should ideally be tested any time it is deployed in a new national 
context (Swami & Barron, 2019).
These limitations notwithstanding, the results of the present study 
suggest that the BrAS is a reliable and valid tool for the assessment of 
breast appreciation in Lebanese women. The availability of this trans­
lation of the BrAS adds to the tools available to researchers in Lebanon 
who are interested in issues related to positive body image, which in 
turn may spur more in-depth understandings of this construct in the 
context of the Middle East and Arabic-speaking communities. For body 
image scholars more broadly, the present results suggest that it may be 
possible to capture a common latent construct of breast appreciation 
across linguistic and national groups using the BrAS. This raises inter­
esting questions about the extent to which the BrAS will evidence 
measurement invariance, which could be assessed in future cross- 
national research. Such research can be expected to contribute to 
more holistic understandings of the positive body image construct, as 
well as fuller consideration of positive breasted experiences across cul­
tural communities.
